issues.description: hash_re='#([0-9]+)' is too broad and will match issue references (e.g. 'issue #52'), step counts ('step #3'), commit refs, etc. Because the three patterns are OR-chained and hash_re is last, it fires whenever pr_re and bang_re don't match. Downstream at line 2707, the extracted number is consumed as the real PR number; the only guard (line 2716) rejects empty/null but not a valid-looking-but-wrong integer. Remove hash_re entirely or restrict it to a clearly PR-context pattern to prevent the review loop from targeting the wrong PR.

grep -oE '\[[ ]*\{[^]]+\]' uses [^]]+ which stops at the first ']' character, silently truncating any task object that contains a nested array (e.g. a 'dependencies' field). The subsequent jq validation then fails and nothing is extracted. Given the orchestrator's own task schemas include nested arrays (see lines 347-358), this will commonly produce no output for real payloads. Replace with a balanced-bracket parser (e.g. a small python3 -c snippet) or explicitly document the limitation.

No negative test verifies that a result text containing a bare '#N' reference (e.g. 'Implemented issue #52') does NOT set pr_number. This is the exact scenario that would catch the hash_re false-positive bug. Add a test: result text contains '#52' in an issue-reference context and assert pr_number is empty/null in the fallback output.

New timeout() mocks do not call 'shift' to skip the timeout argument, unlike the reference test '@test run_stage extracts structured_output' which does. Functionally harmless since mock bodies ignore all args, but inconsistent with the established convention in the test file.

New @test blocks (lines 169–262) use hard tab indentation while all pre-existing tests in the file use 4-space indentation. Functionally harmless but violates the established convention and causes noisy diffs.

New fail() helper writes to stderr (>&2) while peer assertion helpers like assert_contains() write to stdout. Both work in bats failure output, but the inconsistency will confuse future contributors about the expected output channel for assertion helpers.

Suggestion: The balanced-bracket parser requires python3 with no guard. If python3 is absent, 2>/dev/null silently suppresses the error and tasks_match is empty (graceful degradation), but there is no signal to the operator. A brief inline comment or command -v python3 guard would improve transparency.

Suggestion: bang_re='!([0-9]+)' has no leading word boundary or context, so it could match '!42' in unlikely strings like 'Error!42 retries'. Adding a leading anchor (e.g. (^| )!([0-9]+) with BASH_REMATCH[2]) would eliminate false positives, though the current pattern is acceptable for realistic PR-creation output.
